approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursive
approaches.summary: Try all possible parenthesizations recursively by placing parentheses at every possible position between matrices. This explores the entire solution space but is extremely inefficient for larger inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursive Matrix Chain Multiplication
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution that tries all possible parenthesizations to find minimum scalar multiplications.
ladders.explanation: The Matrix Chain Multiplication problem asks: given a sequence of matrices with compatible dimensions (where matrix i has dimensions p[i-1] × p[i]), find the optimal parenthesization that minimizes the total number of scalar multiplications. The brute force recursive approach explores every possible way to parenthesize the chain by recursively trying all split points between matrices. For a chain of n matrices, there are Catalan number of possible parenthesizations, leading to exponential time complexity. The intuition is that at each recursive step, we try placing parentheses at every possible position between matrices, computing the cost of multiplying the left and right subchains, plus the cost of multiplying their results. This creates a binary tree of recursive calls where each node represents a subproblem of multiplying matrices from i to j. The base case occurs when we have only one matrix (cost = 0) or when i = j. The algorithm computes cost = min over all possible k (split points) of: cost(i,k) + cost(k+1,j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]. This explores the entire solution space but is extremely inefficient due to overlapping subproblems. Time complexity is O(2^n) because each recursive call generates two subproblems, and space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack. Connection to L1: This full solution uses the recursive helper function as its core component. Common mistakes include: forgetting the base case, incorrectly computing the multiplication cost for the final multiplication, or using wrong indices for the dimension array. Real code pattern: def mcm_recursive(p, i, j): if i == j: return 0; min_cost = float('inf'); for k in range(i, j): cost = mcm_recursive(p, i, k) + mcm_recursive(p, k+1, j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost); return min_cost.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Helper Function for Subchain Cost
ladders.desc: Recursive function that computes minimum cost for multiplying matrices from index i to j.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive helper function that solves the subproblem: given dimension array p and indices i,j (1-based), compute the minimum scalar multiplications needed to multiply matrices from i to j. The function implements the recurrence relation: if i == j, return 0 (single matrix, no multiplication needed). Otherwise, initialize min_cost to infinity, then iterate through all possible split points k from i to j-1. For each k, recursively compute cost(i,k) + cost(k+1,j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]. The term p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j] represents the cost of multiplying the two resulting matrices from the left and right subchains. Take the minimum over all k. The intuition is that to multiply matrices i through j, we must choose where to place the outermost parentheses, which splits the chain into two parts. We try all possible splits and pick the cheapest one. This creates a binary tree of recursive calls. Time complexity for this subproblem alone is exponential O(2^(j-i+1)) because each call generates two recursive calls. Space complexity is O(j-i+1) for recursion depth. Connection to L0: This function is called by the main solution with i=1, j=n-1. Connection to L2: This function implements the core recurrence relation. Common mistakes: Using 0-based indexing incorrectly (the dimension array p has length n where p[k] gives columns of matrix k+1), forgetting to add the final multiplication cost, or not handling the base case properly. Real code pattern: def solve(p, i, j): if i >= j: return 0; min_cost = float('inf'); for k in range(i, j): cost = solve(p, i, k) + solve(p, k+1, j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost); return min_cost.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recurrence Relation for Optimal Parenthesization
ladders.desc: The mathematical recurrence that defines optimal cost in terms of subproblems.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of Matrix Chain Multiplication is that the optimal cost can be expressed recursively: For matrices i through j (1-indexed), let m[i,j] be the minimum scalar multiplications needed. Then m[i,j] = 0 if i = j (single matrix). Otherwise, m[i,j] = min over k from i to j-1 of { m[i,k] + m[k+1,j] + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j] }. Here p is the dimension array where matrix i has dimensions p[i-1] × p[i]. The term p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j] represents the cost of multiplying the two resulting matrices from the optimal parenthesizations of the left chain (i to k) and right chain (k+1 to j). The intuition is that any parenthesization must have an outermost multiplication, which corresponds to some split point k. We don't know which k is optimal, so we try all possibilities and take the minimum. This recurrence exhibits optimal substructure: an optimal solution to the full problem contains optimal solutions to subproblems. It also has overlapping subproblems, which makes memoization/dynamic programming effective. Time complexity if computed naively is exponential due to repeated computations. Space needed is O(n^2) to store all m[i,j] values. Connection to L1: This recurrence is implemented by the recursive helper function. Connection to L3: This builds upon understanding matrix multiplication dimensions. Common mistakes: Confusing indices (especially the p array indexing), forgetting that p[i-1] gives rows of first matrix in chain, or misunderstanding that the cost term uses dimensions of the matrices being multiplied at the split. Real code pattern: The recurrence is: if i == j: return 0; else: return min( for k in range(i, j): m[i][k] + m[k+1][j] + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j] ).

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Cost of Multiplying Two Resulting Matrices
ladders.desc: Calculate scalar multiplications needed to multiply two matrices given their dimensions.
ladders.explanation: Given two matrices A (dimensions: a × b) and B (dimensions: b × c), the number of scalar multiplications required to compute A × B is a × b × c. This is because each element of the resulting a × c matrix requires b multiplications (dot product of row from A and column from B), and there are a × c elements total. In the context of matrix chain multiplication, when we have optimally parenthesized a left chain resulting in matrix L (dimensions: p[i-1] × p[k]) and a right chain resulting in matrix R (dimensions: p[k] × p[j]), multiplying L × R requires p[i-1] × p[k] × p[j] scalar multiplications. This term appears in the recurrence relation. The intuition is straightforward: matrix multiplication is defined such that if A is m×n and B is n×p, then AB is m×p and each element requires n multiplications. This building block is fundamental because every parenthesization ultimately comes down to multiplying two matrices at each step. Time complexity for this operation is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: This cost term is used in the recurrence relation's p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j] part. Connection to L4: This uses basic multiplication of three numbers. Common mistakes: Confusing which dimensions to use (especially when indices are off by one), or misunderstanding that the middle dimension must match for multiplication to be valid. Real code pattern: cost = rows_of_first * common_dimension * columns_of_second, or specifically: cost = p[i-1] * p[k] * p[j].

examples.trace: Rows of A = 10, columns of A = 30 (which equals rows of B), columns of B = 5. Scalar multiplications = 10 × 30 × 5 = 1500.
Rows of A = 30, columns of A = 5 (which equals rows of B), columns of B = 60. Scalar multiplications = 30 × 5 × 60 = 9000.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through All Possible Split Points
ladders.desc: Loop through all valid positions to place parentheses between matrices.
ladders.explanation: Given a chain of matrices from index i to j (inclusive), there are j-i possible positions to split the chain into two subchains. Specifically, for k ranging from i to j-1, splitting at k means parenthesizing as (matrices i through k) × (matrices k+1 through j). This operation is fundamental because the brute force algorithm must try every possible split to find the optimal one. The loop iterates through each possible k, computes the cost for that split, and keeps track of the minimum. The intuition is that we're exploring all binary tree structures where the root represents multiplication at split k. For n matrices, there are n-1 possible split points for the outermost multiplication. In recursion, this creates an exponential number of possibilities because each subchain itself can be split in multiple ways. Time complexity for this loop in isolation is O(n) where n = j-i+1. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This loop is where we compute the cost term for each split. Connection to L5: This uses basic loop constructs. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in loop range (should be for k in range(i, j) not including j), or confusing split points with matrix indices. Real code pattern: min_cost = float('inf'); for k in range(i, j): cost = ...; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost).

examples.trace: For matrices 1,2,3: Possible splits: k=1 gives (1)×(2,3); k=2 gives (1,2)×(3). Loop would iterate k=1 and k=2.
For matrices 2,3,4: Possible splits: k=2 gives (2)×(3,4); k=3 gives (2,3)×(4). Loop would iterate k=2 and k=3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Matrix Dimensions Array
ladders.desc: Represent matrix chain dimensions using a single array.
ladders.explanation: In Matrix Chain Multiplication, we're given n matrices. Instead of listing each matrix's dimensions separately, we use an array p of size n+1 where p[i-1] and p[i] give the dimensions of matrix i. Specifically, if matrix i has dimensions rows × columns, then rows = p[i-1] and columns = p[i]. This compact representation works because consecutive matrices must have compatible dimensions: the columns of matrix i must equal the rows of matrix i+1, so p[i] serves as both. For example, with matrices: A1: 10×30, A2: 30×5, A3: 5×60, we have p = [10, 30, 5, 60]. Here p[0]=10 (rows of A1), p[1]=30 (columns of A1 = rows of A2), p[2]=5 (columns of A2 = rows of A3), p[3]=60 (columns of A3). The intuition is that this array captures all necessary dimension information with minimal storage. This is foundational because all cost calculations use this array. Time and space for this representation are trivial. Connection to L4: This array is used to compute costs during split iteration. Common mistakes: Thinking p has length n (it has n+1), confusing 0-based vs 1-based indexing, or not understanding why p[i] is shared between matrices. Real code pattern: Given matrices with dimensions, construct p such that p[0] = rows of first matrix, and for each subsequent matrix, add its column dimension.

examples.trace: First matrix: rows=10, columns=30 → p[0]=10, p[1]=30. Second matrix: rows=30 (must match previous columns), columns=5 → p[2]=5. So p = [10,30,5].

approaches.name: Approach 2: Memoized Recursion (Top-down DP)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store results of subproblems, avoiding redundant calculations. This dramatically improves efficiency over brute force while maintaining a recursive structure.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Matrix Chain Multiplication with Memoized Recursion
ladders.desc: Complete solution using memoized recursion to find minimum scalar multiplications for matrix chain multiplication.
ladders.explanation: The Matrix Chain Multiplication problem asks: given a sequence of matrices with compatible dimensions (where matrix i has dimensions p[i-1] × p[i]), find the optimal parenthesization that minimizes the total number of scalar multiplications. The memoized recursion approach solves this by breaking the problem into overlapping subproblems and storing their solutions to avoid redundant calculations. Intuition: Any optimal parenthesization must split the chain at some point k, making the total cost equal to the cost of multiplying matrices i..k plus the cost of multiplying matrices k+1..j plus the cost of multiplying those two resulting matrices. By recursively computing these costs for all possible splits and memoizing results, we efficiently find the minimum. Step-by-step: 1) Define a recursive function solve(i, j) that returns the minimum cost to multiply matrices from index i to j. 2) Base case: if i == j, return 0 (single matrix needs no multiplication). 3) Initialize min_cost to infinity. 4) For each possible split point k from i to j-1: compute cost = solve(i, k) + solve(k+1, j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]. 5) Update min_cost if this cost is lower. 6) Store min_cost in memo table and return it. Time complexity: O(n³) due to n² states and n splits per state. Space: O(n²) for memo table. Connection to L1: This uses the key recursive function with memoization. Common mistakes: forgetting to add the cost of multiplying the two resulting matrices (p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]), incorrect base case, or not handling 1-based indexing properly. Real code pattern: def mcm_memo(p, i, j, memo): if i == j: return 0; if memo[i][j] != -1: return memo[i][j]; min_cost = float('inf'); for k in range(i, j): cost = mcm_memo(p, i, k, memo) + mcm_memo(p, k+1, j, memo) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost); memo[i][j] = min_cost; return min_cost.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Function with Memoization for MCM
ladders.desc: Implement the core recursive function that computes minimum cost for a subchain with memoization.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the memoized recursion approach. It solves the subproblem: given indices i and j (1-based), return the minimum scalar multiplications needed to multiply matrices from i to j. Intuition: The optimal cost for multiplying matrices i..j can be found by trying all possible places to split the chain and combining the costs of the two resulting subchains plus the cost to multiply their results. Memoization stores computed results to avoid recomputation. Step-by-step: 1) Check base case: if i == j, return 0. 2) Check memo table: if result for (i,j) is already computed, return it. 3) Initialize min_cost to a large value. 4) For k from i to j-1: recursively compute cost = solve(i,k) + solve(k+1,j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]. 5) Update min_cost with the minimum cost across all k. 6) Store min_cost in memo table and return it. Time complexity for this function: O(n) per call without memoization, but with memoization, each state (i,j) is computed once, leading to O(n³) overall. Space: O(n²) for memo table. Connection to L0: This function is called recursively in the full solution. Connection to L2: It implements the core logic of trying all splits. Common mistakes: Using 0-based indexing incorrectly (p[i-1] accesses dimension of first matrix in subchain), not initializing memo properly, or missing the multiplication cost term. Real code pattern: def solve(i, j, p, memo): if i == j: return 0; if memo[i][j] != -1: return memo[i][j]; min_cost = float('inf'); for k in range(i, j): cost = solve(i, k, p, memo) + solve(k+1, j, p, memo) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost); memo[i][j] = min_cost; return min_cost.

examples.trace: Compute solve(1,2). Base: i!=j. Check memo: assume empty. min_cost=inf. k from 1 to 1: k=1: cost = solve(1,1)+solve(2,2)+p[0]*p[1]*p[2] = 0+0+10*20*30=6000. min_cost=6000. Store memo[1][2]=6000, return 6000.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Splitting the Chain and Combining Costs
ladders.desc: Understand how to break the matrix chain into subproblems and compute total cost.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of MCM is that any multiplication of a chain of matrices can be viewed as splitting at some point, multiplying the left and right subchains optimally, and then multiplying the two resulting matrices. The total cost is the sum of costs for the two subchains plus the cost to multiply their results. Intuition: This is a divide-and-conquer strategy with overlapping subproblems, making it suitable for dynamic programming. The cost to multiply two matrices of dimensions a×b and b×c is a*b*c scalar multiplications. For a chain from i to j split at k, the cost = cost(i,k) + cost(k+1,j) + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j], where p array stores dimensions. Step-by-step: 1) Identify the chain boundaries i and j. 2) For each possible split point k between i and j-1, consider the split. 3) Compute cost for left subchain (i to k). 4) Compute cost for right subchain (k+1 to j). 5) Add the cost of multiplying the two resulting matrices: p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]. 6) Take the minimum over all k. This logic is applied recursively. Time complexity: Without optimization, exponential due to trying all splits; with memoization, O(n³). Space: O(n²) for storing intermediate results. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the recursive function. Connection to L3: It relies on understanding matrix multiplication dimensions. Common mistakes: Forgetting the multiplication cost term, incorrect indexing for dimensions, or not considering all splits. Real code pattern: min_cost = INF; for k in range(i, j): cost = cost_left + cost_right + p[i-1]*p[k]*p[j]; min_cost = min(min_cost, cost).

examples.trace: Chain: matrices 1 to 2. Only split k=1. cost_left = cost(1,1)=0. cost_right = cost(2,2)=0. Multiply cost: p[0]*p[1]*p[2]=10*20*30=6000. Total=0+0+6000=6000.

Chain: matrices 1 to 3. Splits: k=1: cost_left=cost(1,1)=0, cost_right=cost(2,3)=6000 (from earlier), multiply: p[0]*p[1]*p[3]=40*20*10=8000, total=0+6000+8000=14000. k=2: cost_left=cost(1,2)=24000, cost_right=cost(3,3)=0, multiply: p[0]*p[2]*p[3]=40*30*10=12000, total=24000+0+12000=36000. Minimum=14000.

ladders.title: Building Block — Matrix Multiplication Cost Calculation
ladders.desc: Compute the cost of multiplying two matrices given their dimensions.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on calculating the number of scalar multiplications required to multiply two matrices. Given matrix A of dimensions a×b and matrix B of dimensions b×c, the resulting matrix C is a×c. Each element of C is computed as the dot product of a row from A and a column from B, requiring b multiplications and b-1 additions, but we count only multiplications as they dominate cost. Thus, total scalar multiplications = a * b * c. Intuition: This formula arises because there are a*c elements in C, and each requires b multiplications. Step-by-step: 1) Identify dimensions: a rows and b columns for A, b rows and c columns for B. 2) Verify compatibility: number of columns in A must equal rows in B. 3) Compute cost = a * b * c. This is a fundamental operation used repeatedly in MCM to combine subproblems. Time complexity: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This cost is added when combining subchains. Connection to L4: It involves basic multiplication operations. Common mistakes: Using wrong dimensions, forgetting compatibility check, or miscounting operations. Real code pattern: cost = a * b * c.

examples.trace: Matrix A: 10×20, B: 20×30. Cost = 10 * 20 * 30 = 6000 scalar multiplications.
Matrix A: 40×20, B: 20×10. Cost = 40 * 20 * 10 = 8000 scalar multiplications.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Multiplication
ladders.desc: Access array elements and perform multiplication to compute dimensions.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic operations needed for MCM: accessing elements in an array and multiplying numbers. In MCM, the dimensions of matrices are stored in an array p, where matrix i has dimensions p[i-1] × p[i]. To compute the cost of multiplying matrices from i to j split at k, we need to access p[i-1], p[k], and p[j] and multiply them. Intuition: Proper indexing ensures we get the correct dimensions for the matrices involved. Step-by-step: 1) Understand 1-based indexing for matrices: matrix 1 uses p[0] and p[1], matrix 2 uses p[1] and p[2], etc. 2) For a split at k, the left result has dimensions p[i-1] × p[k], right result has p[k] × p[j]. 3) Multiply these dimensions: p[i-1] * p[k] * p[j] gives the cost. Time complexity: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation computes the multiplication cost. Connection to L5: It relies on understanding arrays and arithmetic. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indexing, using 0-based indexing incorrectly, or integer overflow for large numbers. Real code pattern: cost = p[i-1] * p[k] * p[j].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Understand arrays and perform multiplication operations.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces arrays and basic arithmetic operations, which are prerequisites for MCM. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible by indices. In MCM, we use an array p to store matrix dimensions. Arithmetic operations like multiplication are used to compute costs. Intuition: Arrays allow efficient storage and access of related data; multiplication combines numerical values. Step-by-step: 1) Define an array: e.g., p = [10, 20, 30] means p[0]=10, p[1]=20, p[2]=30. 2) Access elements: use index starting from 0. 3) Multiply numbers: e.g., 10 * 20 = 200. 4) Combine: e.g., p[0] * p[1] * p[2] = 10*20*30=6000. Time complexity: O(1) for access and multiplication. Space: O(n) for array storage. Connection to L4: These operations are used in indexing and cost calculation. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds, misunderstanding zero-based indexing, or arithmetic errors. Real code pattern: arr = [1, 2, 3]; result = arr[0] * arr[1] * arr[2].

approaches.name: Approach 3: Bottom-up Dynamic Programming
approaches.summary: Build solution iteratively using a DP table, starting from smallest subchains and working up to the full chain. This avoids recursion overhead and systematically computes optimal costs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Bottom-up DP for Matrix Chain Multiplication
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of bottom-up DP to find minimum scalar multiplications for matrix chain multiplication.
ladders.explanation: The bottom-up DP approach solves MCM by systematically building solutions from smallest subchains to the full chain. We create a DP table dp[i][j] representing the minimum cost to multiply matrices from index i to j. The key insight is that for any split point k between i and j, the total cost is dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + cost of multiplying the two resulting matrices (p[i]*p[k+1]*p[j+1]). We fill the table diagonally, starting with chains of length 2 (single multiplication) up to length n. For each subchain length L from 2 to n, we consider all possible starting points i, and for each, all possible split points k between i and j (where j = i+L-1). We compute the cost for each split and take the minimum. The answer is dp[0][n-1]. Time complexity is O(n³) due to triple nested loops over chain length, starting positions, and split points. Space complexity is O(n²) for the DP table. This approach avoids recursion overhead and ensures all subproblems are solved exactly once in optimal order.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Computing Minimum Cost for a Subchain
ladders.desc: Given a subchain of matrices from i to j, compute the minimum multiplication cost by trying all split points.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the DP solution. For a fixed subchain from matrix i to j (where j > i), we need to find the optimal split point k (i ≤ k < j) that minimizes total cost. The cost formula is: dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p[i]*p[k+1]*p[j+1]. Here, dp[i][k] is the minimum cost to multiply matrices i through k (already computed for smaller chains), dp[k+1][j] is for matrices k+1 through j, and the last term is the cost to multiply the two resulting matrices. We iterate k from i to j-1, compute each cost, and track the minimum. This works because matrix multiplication is associative—different parenthesizations give the same final matrix but different intermediate scalar multiplication counts. By trying all splits, we guarantee finding the optimal one. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(j-i) per subchain. In the full solution, this is called for each subchain, leading to O(n³) total. Common mistakes include incorrect index handling (e.g., using p[k] instead of p[k+1]) or forgetting that dp[i][i]=0 (single matrix costs nothing). This sub-routine feeds into L0 by being the inner loop that populates each dp[i][j] entry.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Optimal Substructure and Cost Formula
ladders.desc: Understanding why the minimum cost for a chain can be derived from optimal costs of its subchains.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is based on optimal substructure: an optimal parenthesization of a matrix chain must be composed of optimal parenthesizations of its subchains. For matrices i to j, if the optimal split is at k, then the parenthesization is (i...k)(k+1...j), and both parts must be optimal individually. This leads to the recurrence: dp[i][j] = min_{i≤k<j} (dp[i][k] + dp[k+1][j] + p[i]*p[k+1]*p[j+1]). Here, p is the array of dimensions where matrix i has dimensions p[i]×p[i+1]. The term p[i]*p[k+1]*p[j+1] is the cost to multiply the two resulting matrices from the split. Intuition: Multiplying an a×b matrix with a b×c matrix requires a*b*c scalar multiplications. In the split, the left part yields a matrix of size p[i]×p[k+1], and the right part yields p[k+1]×p[j+1], so multiplying them costs p[i]*p[k+1]*p[j+1]. This recurrence works because it considers all possible first-level splits. Time analysis for computing one dp[i][j] is O(j-i). Space for storing results is O(n²). This logic feeds into L1 by providing the formula used in the sub-routine. Common mistakes include misindexing p (e.g., using p[k] instead of p[k+1]) or misunderstanding the dimensions.

examples.trace: Matrix dimensions: A1:10×30, A2:30×5, A3:5×60. For split at k=0: left=(A1) size 10×30, right=(A2×A3) size 30×60, multiply cost=10*30*60. For k=1: left=(A1×A2) size 10×5, right=(A3) size 5×60, multiply cost=10*5*60.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative Table Filling by Chain Length
ladders.desc: Learn to fill a DP table systematically by increasing subchain lengths.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves the iterative process of filling the DP table dp[i][j] in a bottom-up manner. We start by initializing dp[i][i] = 0 for all i (cost of single matrix is 0). Then, we consider chains of increasing length L from 2 to n (where n is number of matrices). For each L, we iterate over all possible starting indices i from 0 to n-L, and set j = i+L-1. For each pair (i,j), we compute dp[i][j] using the recurrence from L2. This order ensures that when computing dp[i][j], all smaller subchains dp[i][k] and dp[k+1][j] (for k between i and j) are already computed because they have length less than L. The intuition is to solve smallest subproblems first and build up to the full problem. Time complexity is O(n³) due to triple loop: O(n) for L, O(n) for i, and O(L) for k. Space is O(n²) for the table. This feeds into L2 by providing the framework where the recurrence is applied. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (e.g., going up to n instead of n-L) or not initializing the diagonal.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Computing Cost of Multiplying Two Matrices
ladders.desc: Calculate the number of scalar multiplications required to multiply two matrices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation computes the cost (number of scalar multiplications) to multiply two matrices. If matrix A has dimensions a×b and matrix B has dimensions b×c, then multiplying A and B requires a*b*c scalar multiplications. The result is a matrix of size a×c. Intuition: Each element of the result is the dot product of a row from A (length b) and a column from B (length b), requiring b multiplications and b-1 additions, but we count only multiplications as they dominate cost. There are a*c elements total, so total multiplications = a*b*c. This is a fundamental operation because in MCM, when we split a chain, we eventually multiply two resulting matrices, and this cost is added to the subproblem costs. Time complexity is O(1) per multiplication. Space is O(1). This feeds into L3 as the term p[i]*p[k+1]*p[j+1] in the DP recurrence. Common mistakes include using wrong dimensions (e.g., for chain i to j, the left matrix after split has dimensions p[i]×p[k+1], right has p[k+1]×p[j+1], so multiplication cost is p[i]*p[k+1]*p[j+1]).

examples.trace: Matrix A: 10×30, B: 30×5. Cost = 10*30*5 = 1500 scalar multiplications.
Matrix A: 40×20, B: 20×30. Cost = 40*20*30 = 24000 scalar multiplications.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Matrix Dimensions and Chain Representation
ladders.desc: Understand how matrix dimensions are represented and what a chain of matrices means.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the basics of matrix multiplication and chain representation. A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers with rows and columns. If matrix A has dimensions a×b (a rows, b columns) and B has dimensions b×c, they can be multiplied to produce C of dimensions a×c. In a chain of n matrices, we have matrices A1, A2, ..., An, where Ai has dimensions p[i-1] × p[i] for i from 1 to n. Thus, the chain can be represented by an array p of size n+1, where p[i] gives the number of columns of matrix i (and rows of matrix i+1). For example, p = [10, 30, 5, 60] means: A1: 10×30, A2: 30×5, A3: 5×60. The chain multiplication problem asks for the minimum number of scalar multiplications to compute the product A1×A2×...×An, assuming standard matrix multiplication algorithm. This is foundational because the DP approach uses this representation to compute costs. Time and space are not applicable here as it's just representation. This feeds into L4 by providing the dimensions used in the cost calculation. Common mistakes include misindexing p or misunderstanding that p has length n+1 for n matrices.

examples.trace: For n=3 matrices, p has length 4. Matrix 1: dimensions p[0]×p[1] = 10×30. Matrix 2: p[1]×p[2] = 30×5. Matrix 3: p[2]×p[3] = 5×60.
For n=4 matrices, p has length 5. Matrix 1: 40×20, Matrix 2: 20×30, Matrix 3: 30×10, Matrix 4: 10×30.
